concepts.title: Postulates Of Kinetic theory
concepts.conceptBody:
Kinetic Theory of Gases

After knowing the experimental gas laws, it is of interest to develop a theoretical model based on the structure of gases, which can correlates are the experimental facts, fortunately such a theory has been developed (known as the kinetic theory of gases) it is possible to derive an expression (known as the kinetic gas equation) from where all these gas laws can be derived. Essential postulates are:

  • A gas consists of a large number of very small spherical tiny particles, which may be identified with the tne molecules. The molecules of a given gas are completely identical in size, shape and mass.
  • The volume occupied by the molecules is negligible in comparison to the volume of the gas.
  • The molecules are in rapid motion which is completely random. During their motion, they collide with one another and with the sides of the vessel.
  • The laws of elassical mechanies, in particular Newton’s second law of motion, are applicable to the molecules in motion.
  • There are no force of attraction or repulsion amongst the molecules, i.e., they are moving independent of one another.
  • At any instant, a given molecule can have energy, ranging from a small value to a very large value, but the average kinetic energy remains constant on a given temperature, i.e. The average kinetic energy is proportional to the absolute temperature of the gas.

concepts.title: Derivation of KTG
concepts.conceptBody:
Derivation of the Kinetic Theory of Gases

An ideal gas is characterized as a gas that consists of molecules with mass and velocity but no volume. These molecules exhibit no attractive or repulsive forces among themselves or with other matter.

  

Consider a collection of $\text{N}$ identical ideal gas molecules in a container with volume $\text{V}$.

$\text{ force }=\frac{\text{mN}{{\text{c}}^{{\bar{2}}}}}{3\text{x}}$

where ${{\text{c}}^{{\bar{2}}}}=$ mean square velocity $=\frac{\text{c}_{1}^{2}+\text{c}_{2}^{2}+\text{c}_{3}^{2}+\ldots +\text{c}_{1}^{2}+\ldots +\text{c}_{\text{N}}^{2}}{~\text{N}}$

(Note: A bar over a symbol, symbol indicates the mean or average value.)

Pressure is force/area

$\text{P}=\frac{\text{ force }}{\text{ area }}=\frac{\text{mN}{{\text{c}}^{{\bar{2}}}}}{3\text{xyz}}=\frac{\text{mN}{{\text{c}}^{{\bar{2}}}}}{3~\text{V}}$

and rearranging

$\text{PV}=\frac{\text{mN}{{\text{c}}^{{\bar{2}}}}}{3}$ (1)

If $\text{N}$ is Avogadro’s Number, ${{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}$, then $\text{n}=1$ and $\text{PV}=\text{RT}$. Substituting $\text{RT}$ for $\text{PV}$ and ${{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}$ for $\text{N}$ in eq. 1

$\text{RT}=\frac{\text{m}{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}{{\text{c}}^{{\bar{2}}}}}{3}$ (2)

Note: If $\text{T}$ is increased then ${{\text{c}}^{{\bar{2}}}}$ will increase and the volume $\text{V}$ of the container must increase if $\text{P}$ is to remain constant.

concepts.title: Derivation from Kinetic Gas Equation
concepts.conceptBody:
Derivation from the Kinetic Gas Equation

The kinetic gas equation can be used to derive various gas laws and to define expressions for some useful quantities such as the root mean square speed and the average kinetic energy. Before deriving these, it is helpful to write this equation in the following form,

Average kinetic energy $\propto \text{T}$

$\frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{u}}^{{\bar{2}}}}\propto \text{T}\quad \text{ or }\quad \frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{u}}^{{\bar{2}}}}=\text{KT}$

(where$\text{K}$is the proportionality constant)

Introducing this in equation we have

$\text{PV}=\frac{1}{3}\text{mN}{{\text{u}}^{{\bar{2}}}}=\frac{2}{3}~\text{N}\left[ \frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{u}}^{{\bar{2}}}} \right]$

$\text{PV}=\frac{2}{3}\text{NKT}$

Now, we proceed to derive the various gas laws from equation.

Boyle’s Law

The essential conditions for Boyle’s law to be applicable are:

(i) Temperature (T) should remain constant.

(ii) Mass of the gas should remain constant in other words, the total number of molecules (N) remains unchanged.

Under these conditions,

$\text{PV}=\text{ constant or P}\propto \frac{1}{~{{\text{V}}_{1}}}$

Which is the expression for Boyle’s law

Charle’s Law

In this case

(i) Pressure (P) remains fixed.

(ii) Mass of the gas remains unchanged, i.e.,$\text{N}$ is constant with these conditions,

$\text{V}=\left( \frac{2}{3}\frac{\text{NK}}{\text{P}} \right)\text{T i}\text{.e}\text{. V}=(\text{ constant })\text{T}\quad \text{ or }\quad \text{V}\propto \text{T}$

as required by Charle’s law.

Avogadro’s Law

It states that under similar conditions of pressure and temperature, equal volume of all gases contains equal number of molecules. Considering two gases, we have

${{\text{P}}_{1}}~{{\text{V}}_{1}}=\frac{2}{3}~{{\text{N}}_{1}}\text{KT and }{{\text{P}}_{1}}~{{\text{V}}_{2}}=\frac{2}{3}~{{\text{N}}_{2}}\text{K}{{\text{T}}_{2}}$

Since ${{\text{P}}_{1}}={{\text{P}}_{2}}$ and ${{\text{T}}_{1}}={{\text{T}}_{2}}$, therefore

$\frac{{{\text{P}}_{1}}~{{\text{V}}_{1}}}{{{\text{P}}_{2}}~{{\text{V}}_{2}}}=\frac{(2/3){{\text{N}}_{1}}\text{K}{{\text{T}}_{1}}}{(2/3){{\text{N}}_{2}}\text{K}{{\text{T}}_{2}}}\Rightarrow \frac{{{\text{V}}_{1}}}{~{{\text{V}}_{2}}}=\frac{{{\text{N}}_{1}}}{~{{\text{N}}_{2}}}$

If volumes are identical, obviously ${{\text{N}}_{1}}={{\text{N}}_{2}}$.

concepts.title: Kinetic Energy of Translation
concepts.conceptBody:
Kinetic Energy of Translation

The average kinetic energy of translation per molecule in a collection of Avogadro’s Number of molecules is

Average Kinetic Energy per Molecule $=\bar{\varepsilon }=\frac{\text{m}{{\text{c}}^{{\bar{2}}}}}{2}$

The kinetic energy of translation for Avogadro’s Number of molecules is

Kinetic Energy of Translation per Mole $={{\text{E}}_{\text{T}}}={{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}\bar{\bar{\varepsilon }}=\frac{\text{m}{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}{{\text{c}}^{{\bar{2}}}}}{2}$

Substituting 3RT for $\text{m}{{\text{N}}_{\text{A}}}{{\text{c}}^{{\bar{2}}}}$, gives ${{\text{E}}_{\text{T}}}=\frac{3\text{RT}}{2}$

The average kinetic energy of translation per molecule is

$\bar{\varepsilon }=\frac{{{E}_{T}}}{{{N}_{A}}}=\frac{3RT}{2{{N}_{A}}}=\frac{3kT}{2}$

where $k$, call Boltzmann’s constant, is the ideal gas constant per molecule.

$\text{k}={{1.38.10}^{-23}}\frac{\text{J}}{\text{ molecule }-\text{K}}$

concepts.title: Root mean square speed
concepts.conceptBody:
The root mean square speed 

$\left( {{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{rms}}} \right)$: This is the type of speed we used in kinetic gas equation, $\text{PV}=\frac{1}{3}\text{mn}{{\text{c}}^{2}}$. It is the hypothetical speed possessed by the all the gas molecules when the total kinetic energy is equally distributed amongst them. Total kinetic energy of a sample containing $n$ number of molecules

$=\frac{1}{2}\text{mc}_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\text{mc}_{2}^{2}+\ldots \ldots +\frac{1}{2}\text{mc}_{\text{n}}^{2}$

If velocity possessed by all the molecules is same and that is c,

Total kinetic energy $=\text{n}\times \frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{c}}^{2}}$

$\text{n}\times \frac{1}{2}\text{m}{{\text{c}}^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}\text{mc}_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\text{mc}_{2}^{2}+\ldots \ldots .+\frac{1}{2}\text{mc}_{\text{n}}^{2}$

${{c}^{2}}=\frac{c_{1}^{2}+c_{2}^{2}+\ldots ..+c_{n}^{2}}{n}$

$\text{c}=\sqrt{\frac{\text{c}_{1}^{2}+\text{c}_{2}^{2}+\ldots ..+\text{c}_{\text{n}}^{2}}{\text{n}}}$

Thus RMS velocity is defined as the square root of the mean of the squares of the speeds of all the molecules present in the given sample of the gas. The value of $\text{c}$ is determined by using the following expressions.

$\text{c}=\sqrt{\frac{3\text{PV}}{\text{M}}}=\sqrt{\frac{3\text{RT}}{\text{M}}}=\sqrt{\frac{3\text{P}}{\text{d}}}$

In applying any of these equations care must be taken to use consistent units. If SI units are used for $\text{R}\left( =8.31\text{kg}{{\text{m}}^{2}}/\left( {{\text{s}}^{2}}\text{kmol} \right),\text{T}(\text{K}) \right.$ and $\text{M}(\text{kg}/\text{mol})$, the rms speed will be in meters per second.

concepts.title: Average speed
concepts.conceptBody:
Average speed $\left( {{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{av}}} \right)$: 

It is arithmetic mean of various speeds of the molecules.

Average speed $\bar{c}=\frac{{{c}_{1}}+{{c}_{2}}+\ldots .+{{c}_{n}}}{n}$

It is equal to $\sqrt{\frac{8\text{RT}}{\pi \text{M}}}$

concepts.title: Most Probable speed
concepts.conceptBody:
Most probable speed $\left( {{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{mp}}} \right)$: 

This is defined as the speed possessed by maximum number of a gas at a given temperature.

It is equal to $\sqrt{\frac{2RT}{M}}$

The three kinds of molecular speeds are related to each other as:

Most probable speed: Average speed: RMS speed

$=\sqrt{2}:\sqrt{\frac{8}{\pi }}:\sqrt{3}$

${{\text{C}}_{\text{mp}}}:{{\text{C}}_{\text{av}}}:{{\text{C}}_{\text{rms}}}:1:1.128:1.224$
